steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks what the author would agree about Tokyo's "high population density." The passage argues that other cities cannot replicate Tokyo's walkability by copying a single feature like density, because Alfonzo shows many factors (personal preference, perceived safety, etc.) together explain pedestrian habits — "none of these factors in isolation fully explains" them.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'It may increase walkability in Tokyo but is known to reduce walkability in other cities': The passage never says density reduces walkability elsewhere — only that it cannot alone replicate Tokyo's results. This reversal of effect is invented. ✗
  • Choice B — 'It should be understood as just one of several factors that influence pedestrian activity in Tokyo': Matches the passage exactly. The author says density is one associated feature among many interdependent factors; no single factor explains pedestrian habits in isolation. ✓
  • Choice C — 'It affects walking decisions in Tokyo less than personal preference and perceived safety do': The passage lists these as examples of important factors but never ranks them or says density matters less than others. This comparison is unsupported. ✗
  • Choice D — 'It is better understood as an effect of the high level of pedestrian traffic in Tokyo than as a cause': Flips the causal arrow. The passage treats density as a feature associated with walkability (an input), not as something that pedestrian traffic produces. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
